attack_chain.description: Bootstrap the attack session using the leaked long-term IAM access key for lab-infra-admin extracted from the terraform.tfstate object in dangerdev-leaked-creds-bucket. Verify identity and confirm AdministratorAccess without calling GetCallerIdentity (per DangerDev opsec pattern).

implementation.method: boto3.Session initialized with LEAKED_KEY_ID and LEAKED_SECRET_KEY from environment; call iam:GetUser and iam:ListAttachedUserPolicies to confirm admin scope

cleanup_actions: iam:UpdateAccessKey + iam:DeleteAccessKey on lab-infra-admin key — done in cleanup_manifest post-emulation
iocs_generated: GetUser + ListAttachedUserPolicies without preceding GetCallerIdentity (DangerDev evasion fingerprint)
API calls from non-corporate IP to IAM global endpoint
GuardDuty: UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/MaliciousIPCaller

attack_chain.description: Enumerate SES sending quota and registered identities using the leaked admin key to assess the account's phishing/spam capacity before committing to persistence.

implementation.method: boto3 SES client (us-east-1) using leaked_admin_key session

iocs_generated: SES enumeration from anomalous IAM principal
Automated API cadence (activity repeated on two separate days two weeks apart per TI)
GuardDuty: Discovery:IAMUser/AnomalousBehavior

attack_chain.description: List all IAM users to map existing accounts for targeting, identify ses-smtp-user naming patterns to inform masquerade design, and spot accounts suitable for access key hijacking (alice.chen).

implementation.method: boto3 IAM client using leaked_admin_key session; store returned username list for masquerade naming and T1098 targeting

iocs_generated: IAM enumeration burst from leaked credential
ListUsers cadence consistent with automated recon tooling
GuardDuty: Discovery:IAMUser/AnomalousBehavior

attack_chain.description: Create IAM user DangerDev@protonmail.me with a console login profile and long-term access key, establishing an independent persistent channel separate from the originally leaked credential. The returned access key is captured as dangerdev_user_key for all subsequent phases.

implementation.method: boto3 IAM client using leaked_admin_key session; capture CreateAccessKey response and store as dangerdev_session credentials in CredentialStore

cleanup_actions: iam:DeleteAccessKey, iam:DeleteLoginProfile, iam:DeleteUser for DangerDev@protonmail.me — executed in T1070 step 15
iocs_generated: Email-format IAM username (DangerDev@protonmail.me) — immediately anomalous
New IAM user created from anomalous source IP
GuardDuty: Persistence:IAMUser/UserPermissions

attack_chain.description: Attach AWS-managed AdministratorAccess policy to DangerDev@protonmail.me. All subsequent API calls pivot to dangerdev_user_key to distance attack activity from the originally leaked credential.

implementation.method: boto3 IAM client using leaked_admin_key session for this final call; CredentialStore switches active session to dangerdev_session after this step

cleanup_actions: iam:DetachUserPolicy removes AdministratorAccess before user deletion in T1070 step 15
iocs_generated: AdministratorAccess attached to newly created non-service IAM user
GuardDuty: PrivilegeEscalation:IAMUser/AdministrativePermissions
AWS Security Hub: IAM.1 — root or admin-equivalent user with active keys

attack_chain.description: Enumerate EC2 regions, running instances, existing security groups, VPCs, and available GPU instance types using DangerDev credentials to survey compute and network capacity before launching mining infrastructure.

implementation.method: boto3 EC2 client (us-east-1) using dangerdev_user_key session

iocs_generated: Broad EC2 enumeration burst from new IAM user (DangerDev@protonmail.me)
GPU instance type filter (p3.*, p4d.*, g4dn.*) — mining candidate reconnaissance
GuardDuty: Discovery:EC2/AnomalousBehavior

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: Launch a t2.micro Windows test instance into the pre-provisioned dangerdev-open-sg security group to validate instance lifecycle before GPU deployment. Terminate the test instance after confirming successful launch. The Pulumi-managed dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance serves as the persistent RDP attack surface; this step emulates DangerDev's t2.micro test-before-GPU pattern.

implementation.method: boto3 EC2 client using dangerdev_user_key session; resolve latest Windows Server 2022 AMI via SSM parameter, run instance, wait for running state, then terminate

cleanup_actions: ec2:TerminateInstances called within this step — test instance terminated before proceeding
iocs_generated: EC2 instance launched by anomalous IAM user (DangerDev@protonmail.me)
RunInstances with security group allowing 0.0.0.0/0 on 3389 and 22
AWS Security Hub: EC2.19 — security group allows unrestricted inbound access
GuardDuty: Backdoor:EC2/C&CActivity (security group pattern)

attack_chain.description: Simulate RDP access to the Pulumi-managed dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance (port 3389 open to 0.0.0.0/0). The attack script performs a TCP SYN probe to port 3389 to confirm reachability and generate VPC Flow Log evidence. No actual RDP handshake or interactive session is established.

implementation.method: Resolve EC2 public IP via ec2:DescribeInstances on dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance, then Python socket.connect_ex((public_ip, 3389), timeout=5). Log OPEN/CLOSED. No RDP client library invoked.
implementation.details: TCP probe is pure Python socket — not a boto3 API call. VPC Flow Logs capture ACCEPT record for the SYN packet on port 3389.

cleanup_actions: TCP probe is stateless — no resources created; VPC Flow Log records are read-only artifacts
iocs_generated: TCP SYN to port 3389 on EC2 public IP — ACCEPT record in VPC Flow Logs
Inbound connection on RDP port from non-corporate foreign IP
GuardDuty: UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/RDPBruteForce (if repeated)

attack_chain.description: USERDATA ONLY — Benign CPU-bound workload runs at instance boot via UserData PowerShell on dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance, simulating cryptomining process lifecycle. Attack script does NOT implement this step — it runs automatically at EC2 launch. GPU instances (p3.16xlarge) excluded per cannot_safely_emulate constraint; t2.micro substituted.

implementation.method: UserData PowerShell on dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance (see userdata_actions in infra plan). 5-minute Math.Sqrt CPU loop then EventLog write. Not called by attack.py.
implementation.details: Observable via CloudWatch CPUUtilization spike on dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance and Windows Application EventLog EventId 9999 from source 'Application'. No real mining binary installed or executed.

cleanup_actions: Workload self-terminates after 5 minutes; no persistent resources created by UserData script
iocs_generated: CPU spike on EC2 instance at launch (CloudWatch CPUUtilization)
Windows Application EventLog EventId 9999 — T1496 simulation marker
GuardDuty: CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B (simulated via CPU pattern)

attack_chain.technique_name: Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location
attack_chain.description: Create IAM user 'ses' to blend with ses-smtp-user.* auto-generated SMTP credential accounts. List and inspect the two pre-provisioned backdoor roles (AWSeservedSSO_AdminAccess and AWSLanding-Zones-ConfigRecorderRoles) to verify masquerade naming is present in the account. Capture ses user access key as ses_user_key.

implementation.method: boto3 IAM client using dangerdev_user_key session

cleanup_actions: iam:DeleteAccessKey for ses user key, iam:DeleteUser for ses — executed in T1070 step 15
iocs_generated: IAM username 'ses' — single-word, matches SES service name, designed to blend with ses-smtp-user.* pattern
AWSeservedSSO_AdminAccess present in account (lowercase 'e' vs legitimate AWSReservedSSO_ prefix — one character diff)
AWSLanding-Zones-ConfigRecorderRoles with AdministratorAccess (mismatched policy for a Config recorder role)
AWS Security Hub: IAM.1

attack_chain.description: Attach AdministratorAccess to both pre-provisioned cross-account backdoor roles and attempt sts:AssumeRole to verify trust policy is live. AssumeRole is expected to return AccessDenied when invoked from the victim account (not the adversary account) — this is documented as EXPECTED_FAILURE confirming the cross-account trust is correctly wired.

implementation.method: boto3 IAM and STS clients using dangerdev_user_key session; catch ClientError code=AccessDenied on AssumeRole and log as EXPECTED_FAILURE
implementation.notes: AttachRolePolicy is idempotent — safe to call on Pulumi-managed roles that already have the policy. sts:AssumeRole from victim account returns AccessDenied (expected) because trust policy requires arn:aws:iam::ADVERSARY_LAB_ACCOUNT_ID:root principal. Script catches botocore.exceptions.ClientError where response['Error']['Code'] == 'AccessDenied' and records EXPECTED_FAILURE with CloudTrail eventName=AssumeRole for detection validation.

cleanup_actions: iam:DetachRolePolicy for both roles (AWSeservedSSO_AdminAccess and AWSLanding-Zones-ConfigRecorderRoles) — done in cleanup_manifest before pulumi destroy
iocs_generated: Cross-account trust role with AdministratorAccess attached — backdoor survives user deletion
AssumeRole attempt logged in CloudTrail even on AccessDenied
GuardDuty: Persistence:IAMUser/UserPermissions
GuardDuty: PrivilegeEscalation:IAMUser/AdministrativePermissions

attack_chain.description: Create a second access key on existing victim account alice.chen and reset her console password via UpdateLoginProfile. This ensures persistent access after DangerDev@protonmail.me is deleted — the hijacked alice.chen credentials survive the T1070 cleanup. Captures alice_hijacked_key for documentation.

implementation.method: boto3 IAM client using dangerdev_user_key session; capture CreateAccessKey response as alice_hijacked_session in CredentialStore

cleanup_actions: iam:DeleteAccessKey for alice.chen TA-created key — done in cleanup_manifest; iam:UpdateLoginProfile to restore alice.chen password to ChangeMe2024!
iocs_generated: alice.chen now has 2 active access keys — Security Hub IAM.3
Password reset on alice.chen performed by DangerDev@protonmail.me session (cross-user manipulation)
GuardDuty: Persistence:IAMUser/UserPermissions

attack_chain.description: Enumerate all S3 buckets and list objects in dangerdev-prod-data-archive to confirm presence of sensitive-looking files (customers/export_2024_q4.csv, backups/db_dump_prod.sql.gz). Also enumerate IAM instance profiles and alice.chen SSH keys — matches DangerDev's observed enumeration cluster.

implementation.method: boto3 S3 and IAM clients using dangerdev_user_key session

iocs_generated: S3 bucket enumeration from anomalous IAM user
GuardDuty: Discovery:S3/AnomalousBehavior
GuardDuty: Discovery:S3/MaliciousIPCaller
S3 data event (ListObjects) on dangerdev-prod-data-archive in CloudTrail

attack_chain.description: Review active GuardDuty findings to assess detection exposure before initiating cleanup. Use SimulatePrincipalPolicy to probe SSM and Secrets Manager permissions without calling those services directly — avoids triggering SSM/SecretsManager-specific detection while still probing access scope. Mirrors DangerDev's use of RDS console user-agent for GuardDuty access.

implementation.method: boto3 GuardDuty and IAM clients using dangerdev_user_key session; set User-Agent to anomalous value via botocore event_system to replicate DangerDev's RDS console user-agent fingerprint

iocs_generated: GuardDuty API access from anomalous IAM principal with anomalous user-agent
SimulatePrincipalPolicy probing SSM and Secrets Manager — permission check without service telemetry
SIEM alert: iam:SimulatePrincipalPolicy targeting sensitive service actions from non-ops principal

attack_chain.description: Delete all DangerDev@protonmail.me and ses user artifacts (access keys, login profiles, policy attachments, users) to erase visible attack footprint — mirrors DangerDev's observed self-cleanup pattern. Script switches to lab_operator_key (LAB_OPERATOR_KEY env var) for final iam:DeleteUser on DangerDev@protonmail.me since an IAM user's own session cannot delete the user account while it holds active credentials.

implementation.method: boto3 IAM client; first uses dangerdev_user_key for ses cleanup, then switches to LAB_OPERATOR_KEY boto3 session for DangerDev self-deletion
implementation.notes: Last three calls (DetachUserPolicy, DeleteAccessKey, DeleteLoginProfile, DeleteUser on DangerDev@protonmail.me) use LAB_OPERATOR_KEY session. CredentialStore marks dangerdev_session as invalidated after delete_user succeeds.

cleanup_actions: All DangerDev@protonmail.me and ses artifacts deleted within this step — no further cleanup needed for these resources
iocs_generated: High-frequency IAM deletion burst (DeleteUser + DeleteAccessKey + DetachUserPolicy in rapid succession)
DangerDev@protonmail.me self-cleanup — cover-up attempt is itself a forensic indicator
SIEM: successive DeleteUser + DetachUserPolicy events from same session
CloudTrail deletions are fully logged — cover-up fails to erase CloudTrail history

attack_chain.description: DOCUMENTED ONLY — Cannot safely emulate: route53domains:RegisterDomain creates real internet-facing infrastructure with billing and legal implications. Attack script logs the CloudTrail-equivalent event structure for RegisterDomain without executing the API call. In the real DangerDev incident, four PayPal-mimicking domains were registered for credential harvesting.

implementation.method: SIMULATED: Script prints structured JSON matching CloudTrail RegisterDomain event format; no boto3 API call is made

cleanup_actions: No API call executed — no cleanup required
iocs_generated: route53domains:RegisterDomain from anomalous principal (simulated in script output only)
AWS billing alert on unexpected Route53 domain charges (would trigger in live execution)

attack_chain.description: DOCUMENTED ONLY — Cannot safely emulate: ses:SendEmail to real external recipients is illegal and harms third parties. Script calls ses:VerifyEmailIdentity on the lab-controlled identity and logs the SendEmail CloudTrail event structure. SES sandbox mode is enforced on the lab account — any accidental SendEmail call would be blocked from external delivery.

implementation.method: boto3 SES client using alice_hijacked_key session; VerifyEmailIdentity is called; SendEmail structure is logged to stdout without API execution

iocs_generated: SES email identity created from anomalous IAM principal (alice.chen hijacked session)
SendEmail API call from SES sandbox account — external delivery blocked, CloudTrail event still generated
AWS Trust & Safety abuse notification pattern (would trigger in live SES production mode)

credential_chain.source: Long-term IAM access key for lab-infra-admin (AdministratorAccess) embedded as plaintext in infra/prod/terraform.tfstate JSON in dangerdev-leaked-creds-bucket — simulates accidentally exposed credential in external storage
credential_chain.expiration: long-term (no expiry — rotated and deleted in cleanup_manifest post-emulation)

credential_chain.source: iam:CreateAccessKey called on DangerDev@protonmail.me during T1136.003 step 4; returned AccessKeyId and SecretAccessKey stored in CredentialStore as dangerdev_session and used for all Phase 2 and Phase 3 API calls

iam:CreateAccessKey called on masquerade user 'ses' during T1036.005 step 10; captured in CredentialStore as ses_masquerade_session — not used for further API calls within attack script (documents DangerDev pattern of creating keys on masquerade accounts)

sts:AssumeRole targeting AWSeservedSSO_AdminAccess role from adversary lab account (ADVERSARY_LAB_ACCOUNT_ID). Returns AccessDenied when called from victim account in emulation — real execution requires adversary account credentials not present in attack.py
credential_chain.expiration: short-term (~1 hour, renewable from adversary account)

credential_chain.source: iam:CreateAccessKey called on existing victim user alice.chen during T1098 step 12; represents post-cleanup persistent access surviving DangerDev@protonmail.me deletion — used for documented-only steps 16 and 17

script_manifest.description: Single unified attack script managing all credential sessions via a CredentialStore dict keyed by credential ID. Initializes leaked_admin_session from LEAKED_KEY_ID + LEAKED_SECRET_KEY env vars. Transitions active session to dangerdev_session after step 5. Captures ses_user_key and alice_hijacked_key as returned credentials from CreateAccessKey calls. Switches to LAB_OPERATOR_KEY boto3 session only for final DangerDev user self-deletion in step 15. Steps 16-17 are documented-only and output simulated CloudTrail JSON without executing API calls.

phases.name: Persistence Hardening, Collection, Evasion, and Documented Phishing

cleanup_manifest.pulumi_managed_resources: All infrastructure destroyed by pulumi destroy — dangerdev-sandbox-vpc, dangerdev-internet-gateway, dangerdev-public-subnet, dangerdev-open-sg, dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance, dangerdev-ec2-keypair, dangerdev-ssm-instance-role, dangerdev-ec2-instance-profile, dangerdev-exposed-admin-user, dangerdev-leaked-creds-bucket, dangerdev-decoy-user-alice, dangerdev-decoy-user-ops, dangerdev-honey-iam-user, dangerdev-ses-email-identity, dangerdev-cross-account-backdoor-role, dangerdev-configrecorder-backdoor-role, dangerdev-sensitive-s3-bucket, dangerdev-secretsmanager-secret, dangerdev-guardduty-detector, dangerdev-cloudtrail, dangerdev-log-bucket
cleanup_order: Revoke dangerdev_session and ses_masquerade_session (if not already deleted by T1070 step 15)
Reset alice.chen console password to original ChangeMe2024! (iam:UpdateLoginProfile)
Deactivate and delete lab-infra-admin leaked key (iam:UpdateAccessKey + iam:DeleteAccessKey)
Confirm no running EC2 instances outside of Pulumi state
